prior_content_md:
**COST ALERT** — FuelEU penalty rates now active from May 2026

FACT: "From 1 May 2026, and in each subsequent year, a company must pay a penalty if a ship: Has exceeded its GHG intensity. Does not meet the RFNBO sub target (as/when applicable). Had had any non-compliant onshore power port calls (from 2030)." *Source: Timetable for Compliance, Lloyd's Register. https://www.lr.org/en/services/statutory-compliance/fueleu-regulation/timetable-for-compliance/

FACT: "The penalties are 2,400 EUR per tonne Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent deficit, with VLSFO serving as the calculation reference due to its market dominance." *Source: The FuelEU Maritime Regulation: Emission Reduction of Fuels in Shipping, carboneer, August 2025. https://carboneer.earth/en/2025/08/fueleu-maritime-compliance-2025/

FACT: "Penalties increase progressively for consecutive non-compliance, creating penalty multipliers of 10%, 20%, 30% and so forth for persistent, yearly violations." *Source: The FuelEU Maritime Regulation: Emission Reduction of Fuels in Shipping, carboneer, August 2025. https://carboneer.earth/en/2025/08/fueleu-maritime-compliance-2025/

FACT: "Onshore power non-compliance incurs penalties of 1.50 EUR per unused kilowatt-hour, calculated against total electrical power demand during applicable berth periods." *Source: The FuelEU Maritime Regulation: Emission Reduction of Fuels in Shipping, carboneer, August 2025. https://carboneer.earth/en/2025/08/fueleu-maritime-compliance-2025/

*Operational implication:* Ocean carriers whose vessels have GHG intensity deficits for 2025 will owe penalties from 1 May 2026. These penalties are expected to translate into surcharges or rate adjustments that flow downstream to freight forwarders and their cargo clients. Workspaces booking ocean freight on EU-touching routes should request from carriers their 2025 compliance balance status and any planned surcharge structures, before the 30 June 2026 Document of Compliance deadline crystallises the financial exposure.

---

**ACTION REQUIRED** — Document of Compliance verification window closes 30 June 2026

FACT: "By 30 June 2026, and in each subsequent year: Verifiers will issue FuelEU DoCs to ships if the ship has no compliance deficit or non-compliant port calls. An EEA administering state will issue FuelEU DoCs for ships that had a compliance deficit, but have paid outstanding penalties." FACT: "Ships subject to FuelEU must carry a valid DoC when entering an EEA port. If a ship fails to present a FuelEU DoC for two or more consecutive reporting periods, they could be banned from EEA ports." *Source: Timetable for Compliance, Lloyd's Register. https://www.lr.org/en/services/statutory-compliance/fueleu-regulation/timetable-for-compliance/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces routing cargo on ocean vessels calling EU ports should confirm by July 2026 that vessels carrying their cargo hold valid FuelEU Documents of Compliance for the 2025 reporting period. Failure of a vessel to hold a valid DoC for two consecutive periods exposes cargo bookings to service disruption risk if the vessel is refused port entry.

---

**MONITORING** — RFNBO sub-target trigger under assessment (re-check: early 2032)

FACT: "The subtarget for RFNBO does not apply if there is evidence, following the Commission's assessment, of insufficient production capacity and availability of RFNBOs, uneven geographical distribution, or a too high price of those fuels, according to Article 5." *Source: Questions and answers on Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime/questions-and-answers-regulation-eu-20231805-use-renewable-and-low-carbon-fuels-maritime-transport_en

FACT: A sub-target of 2% RFNBO from 2034 applies if the Commission determines uptake is below 1% by 2031. *Source: What is FuelEU Maritime? — EU Regulation 2023/1805 Well-to-Wake Guide 2026, Varuna Sentinels BV, April 22, 2026. https://www.varuna-sentinels.com/what-is-fueleu-maritime

*Analytical inference:* The Commission will assess RFNBO uptake data around 2031–2032. The outcome determines whether an additional mandatory 2% RFNBO sub-target triggers from 2034. Workspaces with long-term ocean freight contracts should monitor this trigger; it could materially affect carrier costs and therefore freight rates from 2034 onward.

---

**MONITORING** — OPS obligation for container and passenger ships at TEN-T ports from 1 January 2030

FACT: "From 1 January 2030, container and passenger ships over 5000 GT entering EEA ports must connect to onshore power supplies to meet FuelEU zero-emission at berth requirements at major Trans-European Transport Network (TEN-T) ports." *Source: Timetable for Compliance, Lloyd's Register. https://www.lr.org/en/services/statutory-compliance/fueleu-regulation/timetable-for-compliance/

*Operational implication:* Workspaces booking containerised ocean freight on services calling TEN-T EU ports should note that OPS compliance costs will accrue to carriers from 2030. Port infrastructure readiness varies; non-compliance penalties (€1.50/kWh) become an additional carrier cost driver.

---

## S8.1 GHG Intensity Reduction Targets — The Full Trajectory (Required Slot: Penalty Summary Linked)

FACT: The GHG intensity limit is set as a percentage reduction below the 2020 reference value of 91.16 gCO₂eq/MJ, on a well-to-wake basis. The full time series of reduction targets is:
- "2025: -2%; 2030: -6%; 2035: -14.5%; 2040: -31%; 2045: -62%; 2050: -80%"
*Source: Decarbonising maritime transport – FuelEU Maritime, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime_en

**Qualification — calculation basis:** FACT: The GHG intensity is "calculated on the basis of grams of CO₂ equivalent per megajoule (gCO2eq/MJ) for a calendar year under FuelEU." *Source: Timeline to achieve compliance under FuelEU for the 2025 reporting year, Skuld, 8 April 2026. https://www.skuld.com/topics/environment/air-pollution/europe/timeline-to-achieve-compliance-under-fueleu-for-the-2025-reporting-year/

**Qualification — coverage:** The targets cover "not only CO₂ but also methane and nitrous oxide emissions over the full lifecycle of the fuels used onboard, on a well-to-wake (WtW) basis." This is in contrast to EU ETS, which uses a tank-to-wake approach. *Source: Decarbonising maritime transport – FuelEU Maritime, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime_en

**Qualification — vessel scope for GHG intensity:** Applies to all commercial vessels above 5,000 GT calling at EU ports, regardless of flag. Does not apply to vessel types excluded in Article 2. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Official Journal L 234/48. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng

## S8.2 Compliance Balance Mechanics

FACT: "A positive compliance balance is generated when the vessel has performed better than required under the GHG‑intensity benchmark for that reporting year." FACT: "If the ship's actual GHG-intensity is above the annual FuelEU GHG-intensity benchmark, then the vessel has a negative compliance balance." *Source: Timeline to achieve compliance under FuelEU for the 2025 reporting year, Skuld, 8 April 2026. https://www.skuld.com/topics/environment/air-pollution/europe/timeline-to-achieve-compliance-under-fueleu-for-the-2025-reporting-year/

FACT: "A ship's compliance is always assessed for the whole reporting year." *Source: Timeline to achieve compliance under FuelEU for the 2025 reporting year, Skuld, 8 April 2026. https://www.skuld.com/topics/environment/air-pollution/europe/timeline-to-achieve-compliance-under-fueleu-for-the-2025-reporting-year/

## S8.3 Penalty Provisions (Required Slot: Penalty Summary)

FACT: "The penalties are 2,400 EUR per tonne Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) equivalent deficit, with VLSFO serving as the calculation reference due to its market dominance." *Source: The FuelEU Maritime Regulation: Emission Reduction of Fuels in Shipping, carboneer, August 2025. https://carboneer.earth/en/2025/08/fueleu-maritime-compliance-2025/

FACT: "The penalty is set at €2,400 per tonne of VLSFO energy equivalent, or approximately €58.54 per GJ of non-compliant energy use." *Source: FuelEU Maritime – flexibility mechanisms and pool verification, DNV, 3 December 2025. https://www.dnv.com/news/2025/fueleu-maritime-flexibility-mechanisms-and-pool-verification/

**Qualification — consecutive non-compliance escalator:** FACT: "If a ship has a compliance deficit for two consecutive reporting periods or more, that amount shall be multiplied by 1 + (n -1)/10, where n is the number of consecutive reporting periods for which the company is subject to a FuelEU penalty for that ship." *Source: Timeline to achieve compliance under FuelEU for the 2025 reporting year, Skuld, 8 April 2026. https://www.skuld.com/topics/environment/air-pollution/europe/timeline-to-achieve-compliance-under-fueleu-for-the-2025-reporting-year/

**Qualification — new company exception to escalator:** FACT: "If a ship changes companies the penalty amount will not be multiplied for the first reporting year the vessel is with her new company." *Source: Timeline to achieve compliance under FuelEU for the 2025 reporting year, Skuld, 8 April 2026. https://www.skuld.com/topics/environment/air-pollution/europe/timeline-to-achieve-compliance-under-fueleu-for-the-2025-reporting-year/

**Qualification — OPS non-compliance penalty:** FACT: "Onshore power non-compliance incurs penalties of 1.50 EUR per unused kilowatt-hour, calculated against total electrical power demand during applicable berth periods." *Source: The FuelEU Maritime Regulation: Emission Reduction of Fuels in Shipping, carboneer, August 2025. https://carboneer.earth/en/2025/08/fueleu-maritime-compliance-2025/

**Qualification — penalty payment deadline:** FACT: "Penalties must be paid before 30 June." *Source: Timetable for Compliance, Lloyd's Register. https://www.lr.org/en/services/statutory-compliance/fueleu-regulation/timetable-for-compliance/

**Qualification — penalty destination:** FACT: "Revenues from FuelEU penalties accrue to the EU Innovation Fund and are earmarked for maritime decarbonisation projects." *Source: What is FuelEU Maritime? — EU Regulation 2023/1805 Well-to-Wake Guide 2026, Varuna Sentinels BV, April 22, 2026. https://www.varuna-sentinels.com/what-is-fueleu-maritime

## S8.4 On-Shore Power Supply (OPS) Obligation — Full Trajectory

FACT: "Starting from 1 January 2030, containerships and passenger ships under the scope of Art. 2 FuelEU Maritime Regulation, at berth, moored at the quayside, in a port covered by the Article 9 of the AFIR Regulation must connect to on-shore power supply and use it for all of their electrical power demand at berth. From 1 January 2035 onwards, the same rule applies to ships at berth in any port that develops OPS capacity." *Source: Questions and answers on Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime/questions-and-answers-regulation-eu-20231805-use-renewable-and-low-carbon-fuels-maritime-transport_en

**Qualification — Member State discretion 2030–2034:** FACT: "From 1 January 2030 to 31 December 2034, Member States may apply the obligation for containerships and passenger ships under the scope of Art. 2 FuelEU Maritime Regulation, at berth, moored at the quayside, in a port (or parts of that port) not covered by the Article 9 of the AFIR Regulation." *Source: Questions and answers on Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime/questions-and-answers-regulation-eu-20231805-use-renewable-and-low-carbon-fuels-maritime-transport_en

**Qualification — OPS exceptions (Article 6(5)):** FACT: Exceptions to the OPS obligation include: "(a) Short stays of less than two hours moored at the quayside; (b) When a zero-emission technology is used instead of OPS; (c) Unplanned port calls due to unforeseen circumstances for reasons of safety or saving life at sea; (d) Inability to connect to OPS due to reasons like unavailability of OPS connection points in a port; (e) insufficient available shore-power because of electrical grid stability issues (network provider limitations); (f) incompatibility of OPS connection between on board and shore side equipment (provided that the installation on board of the ship is certified in accordance with technical specifications set out in Annex II of the AFIR)." *Source: Questions and answers on Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime/questions-and-answers-regulation-eu-20231805-use-renewable-and-low-carbon-fuels-maritime-transport_en

**Qualification — 10% cap on port-infrastructure exceptions from 2035:** FACT: "In AFIR ports exceptions provided for in Article 6(5), points (d), (e) and (f) will be limited to: maximum number of port calls corresponding to 10 % of a ship's total number of port calls that took place during a reporting period, rounded up to the nearest whole number, or maximum of 10 port calls during the relevant reporting period, whichever is lower." *Source: Questions and answers on Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime/questions-and-answers-regulation-eu-20231805-use-renewable-and-low-carbon-fuels-maritime-transport_en

## S8.5 Flexibility Mechanisms — Banking, Borrowing, and Pooling

### Banking

FACT: "A vessel with a positive compliance balance in a reporting period can bank all or part of its surplus for subsequent reporting periods in THETIS. Banking is ship-specific, so in subsequent years it can be used to offset deficits of the same vessel." FACT: "The surplus does not expire and follows the ship in case of a change of company." FACT: "If it is not banked in that year, the not-banked portion is lost." *Source: FuelEU Maritime – flexibility mechanisms and pool verification, DNV, 3 December 2025. https://www.dnv.com/news/2025/fueleu-maritime-flexibility-mechanisms-and-pool-verification/

### Borrowing

FACT: "The borrowed amount is limited to 2% of the GHG intensity target for the reporting period year N, multiplied by the ship's total energy consumption." FACT: "The borrowed amount is subject to a 10% increase the following year." FACT: "The company may not borrow only part of the deficit; it must either borrow the entire deficit or not borrow at all." FACT: "Borrowing cannot take place in two consecutive reporting periods and cannot be done simultaneously with pooling in the same reporting period." *Source: FuelEU Maritime – flexibility mechanisms and pool verification, DNV, 3 December 2025. https://www.dnv.com/news/2025/fueleu-maritime-flexibility-mechanisms-and-pool-verification/

**Qualification — borrowing trap if vessel leaves EU trade:** FACT: "If a ship did not call at an EU port during the reporting period, but had borrowed an ACS in the previous period, then the administering state must notify the company by 1 June of the verification period. The notification will include the FuelEU penalty amount that the ship initially avoided by borrowing the ACS. This amount is then multiplied by 1.1." *Source: FuelEU Maritime – flexibility mechanisms and pool verification, DNV, 3 December 2025. https://www.dnv.com/news/2025/fueleu-maritime-flexibility-mechanisms-and-pool-verification/

### Pooling

FACT: "Two or more vessels, including from different companies, can join a pool and allocate the CB between them." FACT: "Ships which enter a pool with a deficit cannot have a higher deficit after allocation. Ships which enter a pool with a surplus cannot have a deficit after the allocation." FACT: "The total pool compliance balance must be positive, as otherwise the pool is not valid." *Source: FuelEU Maritime – flexibility mechanisms and pool verification, DNV, 3 December 2025. https://www.dnv.com/news/2025/fueleu-maritime-flexibility-mechanisms-and-pool-verification/

FACT: "A ship's compliance balance may not be included in more than one pool in the same reporting period." *Source: Timeline to achieve compliance under FuelEU for the 2025 reporting year, Skuld, 8 April 2026. https://www.skuld.com/topics/environment/air-pollution/europe/timeline-to-achieve-compliance-under-fueleu-for-the-2025-reporting-year/

FACT: "Two separate pools may be used for GHG intensity target and for the subtarget for RFNBO." *Source: Timeline to achieve compliance under FuelEU for the 2025 reporting year, Skuld, 8 April 2026. https://www.skuld.com/topics/environment/air-pollution/europe/timeline-to-achieve-compliance-under-fueleu-for-the-2025-reporting-year/

## S8.6 RFNBO Multiplier

FACT: "Renewable Fuels of Non-Biological Origin (RFNBOs — e-ammonia, e-methanol, green hydrogen) count 2× toward the GHG-intensity reduction until 2033. From 2034, the multiplier phases out." *Source: What is FuelEU Maritime? — EU Regulation 2023/1805 Well-to-Wake Guide 2026, Varuna Sentinels BV, April 22, 2026. https://www.varuna-sentinels.com/what-is-fueleu-maritime

## S8.7 Document of Compliance and Expulsion

FACT: "Ships subject to FuelEU must carry a valid DoC when entering an EEA port. If a ship fails to present a FuelEU DoC for two or more consecutive reporting periods, they could be banned from EEA ports." *Source: Timetable for Compliance, Lloyd's Register. https://www.lr.org/en/services/statutory-compliance/fueleu-regulation/timetable-for-compliance/

FACT: "Where a ship fails to comply with the obligation having to present a valid FuelEU document of compliance, for two or more consecutive reporting periods, and where other enforcement measures have failed to ensure compliance with Article 24, the competent authority of the Member State of the port of call may, in respect of a ship not flying the flag of that Member State and after giving the opportunity to the company concerned to submit its observations, issue an expulsion order." FACT: "Every Member State, with the exception of the Member State whose flag the ship is flying, shall refuse entry of the ship which is subject to the expulsion order into any of its ports until the company fulfils its obligations." *Source: Timeline to achieve compliance under FuelEU for the 2025 reporting year, Skuld, 8 April 2026. https://www.skuld.com/topics/environment/air-pollution/europe/timeline-to-achieve-compliance-under-fueleu-for-the-2025-reporting-year/

FACT: "The FuelEU document of compliance shall be valid for a period of 18 months after the end of the reporting period or until a new FuelEU document of compliance is issued, whichever occurs earlier." *Source: Timeline to achieve compliance under FuelEU for the 2025 reporting year, Skuld, 8 April 2026. https://www.skuld.com/topics/environment/air-pollution/europe/timeline-to-achieve-compliance-under-fueleu-for-the-2025-reporting-year/

---

## Decided Questions

**Q1: Does the regulation apply to vessels below 5,000 GT?**
DECIDED — NO. FACT: The regulation "should apply to ships with a gross tonnage (GT) above 5,000." Exclusion applies to warships, naval auxiliaries, fish-catching or fish-processing ships, wooden ships of a primitive build, ships not propelled by mechanical means, and government ships used for non-commercial purposes. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, Official Journal L 234/48. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng

*Operational implication:* Ocean freight carried on feeder vessels or small domestic vessels below 5,000 GT is outside FuelEU scope. This threshold is operationally significant for workspaces whose cargo uses mixed-vessel services where feeder legs are operated by smaller vessels.

**Q2: Does the 100%/50% rule apply based on voyage type or port jurisdiction?**
DECIDED. FACT: 100% applies to intra-EU voyages; 50% applies to international voyages (one EU port, one non-EU port); 50% applies to voyages to/from outermost regions. *Source: Questions and answers on Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime/questions-and-answers-regulation-eu-20231805-use-renewable-and-low-carbon-fuels-maritime-transport_en

**Q3: Does the OPS obligation apply to general cargo/multipurpose vessels carrying some containers?**
DECIDED — NO. FACT: OPS requirements "will apply only to ships carrying exclusively containers." *Source: Questions and answers on Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime/questions-and-answers-regulation-eu-20231805-use-renewable-and-low-carbon-fuels-maritime-transport_en

**Q4: Are Norwegian and Icelandic ports treated as EU ports under FuelEU from 1 January 2025?**
DECIDED — NO (currently). FACT: "Norwegian and Icelandic ports will be regarded as third-country ports in a FuelEU context from 1 January 2025 until the incorporation of FuelEU in the EEA Agreement." *Source: Decarbonising maritime transport – FuelEU Maritime, European Commission DG MOVE, 18 December 2025. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime_en

**Q5: Can pooling occur across different companies?**
DECIDED — YES, with constraint. FACT: "Two or more vessels, including from different companies, can join a pool and allocate the CB between them." However, FACT: "Borrowing cannot be done simultaneously with pooling in the same reporting period." *Source: FuelEU Maritime – flexibility mechanisms and pool verification, DNV, 3 December 2025. https://www.dnv.com/news/2025/fueleu-maritime-flexibility-mechanisms-and-pool-verification/

## Questions Requiring Legal Confirmation

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, in any transaction type where it acts as cargo operator, NVOCC, or slot charterer, is or could be treated as the ISM Company bearing direct FuelEU compliance obligations requires counsel review of each specific commercial arrangement.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether FuelEU penalty pass-through clauses in carrier contracts, charter parties, or service agreements create downstream financial exposure for the workspace in its role as freight forwarder or cargo booker requires counsel review of all relevant contract terms.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the fuel supplier liability provision under Article 10 (where suppliers may bear penalties if certified green fuel fails agreed GHG intensity standards) creates any contractual rights or obligations for the workspace as cargo owner or freight principal requires counsel review.

---

## Defined Compliance Entity

FACT: "The entity responsible for ensuring compliance with FuelEU Maritime Regulation should be the entity that is responsible for compliance of the ship with the International Safety Management Code (ISM Company). This is the company assuming responsibility for the operation of the ship and taking on all the duties and responsibilities imposed by the International Management Code for the Safe Operation of Ships and for Pollution Prevention." *Source: Questions and answers on Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime/questions-and-answers-regulation-eu-20231805-use-renewable-and-low-carbon-fuels-maritime-transport_en

FACT: "In practice, this entity can be either the shipowner, or any other entity organisation and person, distinct from the shipowner. Where a shipowner is responsible for the compliance of its ship with the ISM Code, there is no 'ISM company' distinct from the shipowner." *Source: Questions and answers on Regulation (EU) 2023/1805, European Commission DG MOVE. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/transport-modes/maritime/decarbonising-maritime-transport-fueleu-maritime/questions-and-answers-regulation-eu-20231805-use-renewable-and-low-carbon-fuels-maritime-transport_en

## Workspace Position

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, acting as a freight forwarder arranging ocean transport rather than as a shipowner, ship manager, or bareboat charterer, constitutes the defined "company" (ISM Company) for the purposes of direct compliance obligations under FuelEU Maritime requires counsel review. The regulation's text places compliance obligations on the ISM Company, not on freight forwarders as a category.

## Cost Pass-Through Chain

*Analytical inference:* The established pattern in EU maritime regulation is for the ISM Company to recover compliance costs (penalty exposure, fuel cost differential, surcharges) through freight rates and fuel surcharges imposed on cargo principals and freight forwarders. Workspaces that book ocean freight on EU-touching routes are positioned as downstream recipients of these cost pass-throughs. The channel is: ISM Company compliance cost → carrier rate structures → freight forwarder's client invoicing.

## Charter Party Implications

FACT: "Shipowners must ensure that charter party agreements contain robust, updated clauses that transfer financial responsibility for FuelEU penalties to the charterer who controls fuel selection and trading." *Source: The FuelEU Maritime Regulation: Emission Reduction of Fuels in Shipping, carboneer, August 2025. https://carboneer.earth/en/2025/08/fueleu-maritime-compliance-2025/

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, in any capacity in which it arranges or books ocean transport under charter party or service contract terms, carries contractual exposure for FuelEU compliance costs or penalties through contract clause pass-through mechanisms requires counsel review of each relevant contract.

---
